title: Sadhana Pada
summary: Explains the practice of Kriya Yoga, the five obstacles (kleshas) such as ignorance, egoism, and attachment, and the limbs of Ashtanga Yoga (Yama, Niyama, Asana, Pranayama, Pratyahara). Addresses habits, discipline, physical health, and breath control.

sections.summary: Themes: action and duty, self-knowledge, devotion. The passage moves from “Kriya Yoga comprises of self discipline, study of self, and devotion towards Ishvara.” through “Through meditation, the fluctuations of mind that arise due to these kleshas* (sufferings) can be terminated.” through “Pure awareness (drashta*) is simply the act of seeing (hence called seer), but it typically appears to function through the perceiving mind…”

Themes: action and duty, self-knowledge, devotion. The passage moves from “The purpose of the phenomenal world (drashya*) is essentially to reveal this truth (for the seer).” through “These principles are solemn resolutions. They are not bound by one's social status, territory, specific time or any duration. They are universal.” through “Practicing cleanliness, one becomes free from the body's compulsions and from the need of (physically) associating with others.”

Themes: self-knowledge, devotion, mind and meditation. The passage moves from “Developing sattva through the practice of cleanliness (mental purification) leads to radiance, increased ability to focus, sense control, and…” through “At this point, the dualities of opposites no longer disturbs one.” through “Afterwards, the supreme mastery over the senses is attained.”

verses.translation: Kriya Yoga comprises of self discipline, study of self, and devotion towards Ishvara.
The aim of this path of action is to eliminate the roots of suffering and accomplish samadhi* (an advanced meditative state).
The roots of suffering include - ignorance, the sense of 'I', attachment, aversion, and fear of death.
The causes of suffering germinate due to 'ignorance' and these sufferings can be either dormant, weakened, interrupted or fully activated.
Ignorance is the state where one confuses impermanence, impurity, distress, and non self with permanence, purity, happiness, and self.
The sense of 'I' (wrongly) identifies perception of pure awareness as the perception of the senses.
Attachment is the cause of pleasurable experiences.
Aversion is a cause of suffering(s).
Even the wise similar to a non-wise, instinctively fear death, perpetuating suffering.
These kleshas* (sufferings) are very subtle (hence not easily erased). They are eliminated only when the mind merges into its orgin.
Through meditation, the fluctuations of mind that arise due to these kleshas* (sufferings) can be terminated.
The deposits of karma* (actions) are rooted in the causes of klesha* (suffering), which is experienced either in the future or in the present.
As long as the root source i.e. klesha (suffering) exists, its contents will continue to result as - type of birth, span of life, and experience of life.
Each person's- type of birth, span of life, and experience of life, fructifes either as pleasure or pain, which is resultant of their past virtuous or viceful actions.

verses.sanskrit: परिणाम तापसंस्कार दुःखैर्गुणवृत्तिविरोधाच्च दुःखमेव सर्वं विवेकिनः
verses.translation: The wise recognize that suffering is inherent in all experiences, whether it be the agony of the results of actions, or the pain of distress, or of the burden of latent impressions (samskaras*) filled with suffering, along with the constant struggle of the flutuations of the mind due to the fundamental qualities (gunas*) of nature.
However, it is possible to prevent suffering from arising altogether.
The root cause of all suffering is the mistaken belief that pure awareness and the perception of awareness are inseparable.

The phenomenal world that awareness perceives is characterized by luminosity (sattva), activity (rajas), and inertia (tamas), and is composed of - elements and senses, and serves the purpose of both sensual experience as well as liberation (to drasta*).
The various levels of existence - whether they are differentiated, undifferentiated, distinct or indistinct - are all expressions of the basic characteristics inherent in nature (gunas*).
Pure awareness (drashta*) is simply the act of seeing (hence called seer), but it typically appears to function through the perceiving mind despite its purity.
The purpose of the phenomenal world (drashya*) is essentially to reveal this truth (for the seer).
Once this realization occurs, the phenomenal world ceases to be for them, but remains a common reality for the (non-realized) others.
It is the apparent indivisibility of pure awareness and the phenomenal world (i.e. the concept of conjunction), that gives insight into the potency of both the possessor and the possessed.
The cause of this phenomenon (of conjunction) is ignorance.
When this ignorance fades, the appearance of indivisibility also fades, and it becomes clear that pure awareness is free and unaffected by phenomenal world which the state of liberation (kaivalya*).

Unflinching discriminative discernment (between the seer and the seen) is the path to liberation (not the final state yet, this is the upper stage of Ritambhara pragya (1.48) where even this prayga ceases).
On this path of liberation, the one (yogi) with the wisdom of discriminative discernment extends to seven ultimate stages.
By practicing the components of yoga, impurities diminish, allowing the light of understanding to shine and illuminate the path to discriminative awareness.
Social discipline, personal observances, pyscho-physical posture, breath regulation, withdrawl of senses, concentration, meditation and liberation make up the eight limbs of yoga.
The five social disciplines are non-violence, being truthful, not stealing, practicing celibacy, and avoiding materialism.
These principles are solemn resolutions. They are not bound by one's social status, territory, specific time or any duration. They are universal.
The five personal observances are purifying the body, cultivating contentment, practicing austerity, self-study, and devotion to God.
Negative thoughts can be countered by developing positive ones.

verses.sanskrit: वितर्का हिंसादयः कृतकारितानुमोदिता लोभक्रोधमोहपूर्वका मृदुमध्याधिमात्रा दुःखाज्ञानानन्तफला इति प्रतिपक्षभावनम्
verses.translation: Negative thoughts such as violence etc. arise from greed, anger, or attachment, and regardless of their intensity such as less, medium or excessive, inevitably lead to ignorance and suffering. Therefore, it is necessary to cultivate counteracting (i.e.positive) thoughts.
When one is strongly rooted in non-violence, it fosters an environment (around that yogi) where others resign their hatred.
Those who become firmly rooted in truthfulness, experience every action and its outcome in accordance with the truth.
For those established in the virtue of non-stealing (or not accepting which is not their's), entire treasures are revealed.
Celibacy when deeply ingrained secures energy.
Being resolute in the state of non-greediness (desirelessness), the understanding of (previous) births is revealed.
Practicing cleanliness, one becomes free from the body's compulsions and from the need of (physically) associating with others.
Developing sattva through the practice of cleanliness (mental purification) leads to radiance, increased ability to focus, sense control, and ability for self-awareness.
Contentment brings an unmatched sense of joy.
Through intense discipline, impurities are burned away, and the body and senses become highly refined.
Self-reflection strengthens the connection with one's personal deity.
Devotion towards God or Ishvara* bestows the competence to attain samadhi* (liberation).
The body should actualize both steadiness and ease during a psychophysical posture.
This is possible when all effort is relaxed, and a sense of unity between the body and the infinite universe emerges.
At this point, the dualities of opposites no longer disturbs one.
After the psychophysical posture is achieved, breath regulation follows. It happens when inhalations and exhalations are regulated.

(Breath regulation or Pranayama* has three dimensions) inhalation, exhalation, and the (various) pauses (kumbhakka*). By paying attention to the place, time and number of each aspects, they become more subtle and extended.
In the fourth dimension (of breath regulation), the division between inhaling and exhaling disappears.
Then, the concealer of the illumination (sattva*) fades.
Also, the capability of the mind to concentrate (dharana*) is actualized.
When the senses are disengaged from their respective sense objects and identifies with the nature of the chitta, then sense withdrawal (Pratyahara*) happens.
Afterwards, the supreme mastery over the senses is attained.
